source.title: Emergence of abstract rules in the primate brain

confidence_rationale: Nature Reviews Neuroscience synthesis of single-unit + lesion + imaging evidence specifically on abstract-rule representation in primate PFC. Authors span three major labs (Mansouri, Freedman, Buckley) with complementary methodological strengths. Directly on-point for the question 'where does a lateral-PFC analogue hold the current rule such that MECH-261 can write into it?'
failure_signatures: If abstract-rule neurons were absent in dorsolateral PFC but present in OFC or vmPFC, REE's 'rules go to lateral PFC, values go to vmPFC/OFC' partition would be wrong.
If rule-selective neurons required stimulus-driven updates to persist, the active-maintenance framing that MECH-261 relies on would collapse.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Neurons in primate lateral PFC encode abstract task rules (same/different, match/non-match, category membership) independently of the specific stimuli those rules operate on; rule-selective activity emerges with training and persists across delays.
mapping.ree_translation: Supplies the specific single-unit evidence that the lateral-PFC analogue holds stimulus-abstracted rule representations — not just working memory for the current stimulus. MECH-261 writes into these rule-selective populations during internal_planning and external_task, and must not overwrite them during internal_replay.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Rule-selective neurons have been characterised most thoroughly in non-human primates (macaque). Direct human single-unit data are sparser (limited to epilepsy patients). REE should treat the rule-neuron substrate as well-supported in principle but be cautious about quantitative parameters (proportions, timing) when porting from macaque to a humanised agent.
mapping.source_context: Primate single-unit recording (macaque lateral PFC) + human fMRI + selective-lesion studies.

confidence_components.notes: Source quality very high (NRN, three-lab synthesis). Mapping fidelity is strong because 'abstract rule' in the paper corresponds almost exactly to what REE wants the lateral-PFC analogue to hold. Transfer risk is moderate: the mechanism is well-validated in macaque, less directly in humans, and REE is agnostic about the exact format of the abstract representation.
